summary: I was born and raised in Burma, where life was very hard for my family because we are Karen. When I was about five years old, the Burmese military restricted our movements and made it difficult to get enough food. My family lived in constant fear, especially after an incident when soldiers confronted my mother and me. Eventually, in 2006, my family decided to leave Burma to escape the violence and hardship.

I am part of the Karen people, an ethnic group in Burma who have faced a lot of discrimination and violence from the Burmese military. Growing up, my family and I experienced severe restrictions and hardships because of our identity. Even in the refugee camp in Thailand, we maintained our Karen traditions, like weaving traditional clothes. Being Karen has shaped my life journey from Burma to Thailand and finally to Minnesota in the United States.

The Burmese military caused a lot of fear and suffering for my family and the Karen people in Burma. When I was a child, their soldiers wouldn't let us go outside or feed our animals, and they spoke only Burmese, which my mother didn't understand. One time, soldiers surrounded us and questioned my mother, and I was scared they might kill her. Their oppression was a major reason why my family decided to flee Burma in 2006.

After leaving Burma in 2006, my family and I lived in a refugee camp in Thailand for several years. Life there was difficult; we received free rice and food but had little money for other needs. My father worked hard, sneaking out to find jobs with local Thai people, even though it was illegal, while my mother weaved Karen traditional clothes to help support us. We stayed in Thailand until 2012, when we were accepted for resettlement in the United States.
cues: 2006 moved to refugee camp in Thailand

summary: My family and I arrived in Minnesota on September 29, 2013, not knowing anyone and feeling very lonely at first. We stayed inside and cried every day, but after about a year and a half, I got to know more Karen people here and started feeling better. Minnesota has given me opportunities to learn and attend a great school, and I am grateful that my family can live happily in this state. It feels like a new beginning for us after all the hardships we faced.

The United States opened its doors to refugees like my family who lived in camps in Thailand, allowing us to start a new life here. We applied for resettlement in 2012 and were accepted, arriving in Minnesota in 2013. Living in the U.S. has given me hope and opportunities to learn and grow, and I am very thankful for the chance to live safely and happily with my family. I believe that as long as we are alive, everything will get better here.
